fun_facts.fact: The production team spent over six months researching Elizabethan court architecture to design an Elsinore Castle set that blends 16th-century Danish aesthetics with stark modern minimalism.
The lead actor reportedly read all existing recorded performances of Hamlet — over 40 stage and screen versions — before deciding to play the role entirely without watching any of them again.
Director of photography used only natural light and practical torch fixtures for all interior castle scenes, requiring the cast to rehearse extensively to hit precise marks in near-darkness.
The ghost sequences were filmed in a decommissioned industrial freezer kept at 2°C, so that the actors' visible breath would be entirely real rather than added in post-production.
The screenplay went through 14 drafts over three years, with the screenwriter consulting two Oxford Shakespeare scholars to modernize the language while preserving every major verse structure.
The director has a signature habit of shooting the first take of every scene with zero prior blocking — actors are told only where they enter, and the camera crew improvises around them.

first_act_hint: The film opens in a mood of suffocating grief and uneasy ceremony — think black mourning clothes barely exchanged for wedding attire, cold stone corridors, and a royal court that smiles too quickly. Expect a slow-burn atmosphere of tension and unease before anything supernatural makes itself known.
